title: Bosses failing to offer AI training for workers
full_text:
Almost 80 per cent of workers using artificial intelligence at work have received no formal training from their employers, according to a survey of more than 6500 professionals in Australia and New Zealand.
However, only 22 per cent of professionals and 23 per cent of employers are concerned about their organisation’s ability to adapt to AI and new technologies in the next one to three years, a disconnect that suggests many companies were overestimating their readiness.
The latest Hays Salary Guide, which captures insights from employees and those in hiring roles, found just 30 per cent of professionals were quite or very concerned about the potential risks AI posed to future employment opportunities.
Hays APAC chief executive Matthew Dickason said the research showed employees increasingly understood it was important to develop AI skills and, while employers also recognised this importance, many did not know how to invest and drive enablement in their businesses.
Sixty per cent of those surveyed said they were using AI regularly at work, but 78 per cent reported receiving no formal training from their employers.
Just 13 per cent of organisations said they had broad AI adoption across the organisation; 40 per cent had moderate adoption across teams, while 35 per cent cited early exploration. The greatest barriers cited to AI adoption were security and privacy concerns (48 per cent); a lack of training (45 per cent); and lack of skills (33 per cent).
“Employers are lagging from an investment perspective in enabling AI but expecting employees to make their own way,” Mr Dickason said.
He said AI adoption had “outrun enablement – the tools are on the desk, but the training, governance and guardrails haven’t kept up. This isn’t an AI problem; it’s a workforce planning problem”.
While average wage rises were close to 4 per cent, half of the survey respondents felt underpaid. Three in five professionals earning less than $79,000 reported little to no meaningful salary growth, compared with 36 per cent of those earning above $80,000, highlighting a widening gap in earnings.
Only 12 per cent of professionals reported increases between 6 and 10 per cent in the last year while 42 per cent of employees claimed to have received little to no meaningful increase.
Employers in the legal, consulting and strategy, and accounting and finance sectors expect higher-than-average salary increases in the next year, while retail, hospitality and tourism, education, and the public sector were most pessimistic about their pay packets growing.
TAD-2135 Frontline charts
Bosses expect an average pay rate change of 3.8 per cent over the next 12 months, slightly below last year’s 3.9 per cent.
Although two-thirds of workers felt confident in their job security over the next year, just 53 per cent felt satisfied in their job.
One in five professionals changed employers in the past year, and 38 per cent plan no change in the next 12 months.
“Employers shouldn’t confuse low mobility with low dissatisfaction,” Mr Dickason said.
Only one in five changed jobs last year, yet one in three say there’s no clear promotion structure, and pay growth is only just tracking inflation.
“The conditions for a retention problem are building underneath stable turnover numbers, Workers are staying put out of caution, not contentment.
“Progression pathways remain unclear, pay is largely stagnant in real terms, and underlying dissatisfaction is building quietly.”
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
